none: Environmental Impact Statement, Randolph Urban Renewal Project

The Richmond Redevelopment and Housing Executive Agency is proposing a $25,437,377 project to revitalize the Randolph Street corridor in Richmond, Virginia. This project, overseen by LPA, aims to address urban blight and promote economic growth through the construction of new housing and improvements to existing structures. The project involves demolition and removal of existing buildings, followed by the construction of new residential units and public facilities. The goal is to create a more vibrant and accessible neighborhood, enhancing the quality of life for residents. Funding will be secured through a combination of land donations, project capital grants, and relocation payments. The project is expected to be completed within a timeframe of 12 years.
Environmental Impact Statement, Randolph Urban Renewal Project

The Richmond Redevelopment and Housing Executive Agency is proposing a $25,437,377 project to revitalize the Randolph Street corridor in Richmond, Virginia. This project, overseen by LPA, aims to address urban blight and promote economic growth through the construction of new housing and improvements to existing structures. The project involves demolition and removal of existing buildings, followed by the construction of new residential units and public facilities. The goal is to create a more vibrant and accessible neighborhood, enhancing the quality of life for residents. Funding will be secured through a combination of land donations, project capital grants, and relocation payments. The project is expected to be completed within a timeframe of 12 years.
Timothy Atheson; Philip V. Sanchez; Merlin K. DuVal; James Costantina; James Madison Building; William J. Leidinger

The Randolph Redevelopment project, initiated in 1972, aimed to revitalize a significant portion of Richmond’s historic downtown area by demolishing existing structures and constructing new, modern buildings. The project, driven by a desire to modernize the city’s urban landscape, faced significant challenges due to complex zoning regulations and community opposition. Despite initial optimism, the project ultimately faced substantial cost overruns and delays, leading to a significant reduction in the final budget and a partial completion of the redevelopment plan. Therefore, the project was completed with a reduced scope and a substantial financial loss.
